params.text:
Behaviour has a function

A central idea in PBS is that all behaviour has a function — a purpose or reason, even when that reason is not obvious to us. Behaviour does not come from nowhere, and it is not simply “part of the condition”. It is a person's way of getting a need met or responding to how they feel. Once you understand that behaviour is meaningful, it becomes much easier to respond with patience rather than frustration.

  • Purpose: behaviour is always trying to achieve something for the person.
  • Meaningful: it makes sense from the person's point of view.
  • A message: it tells us something about the person's needs.

Working out the function of a behaviour is a skilled task, led by trained professionals through careful assessment.

params.alt: Illustration for Behaviour has a function

params.text:
Behaviour as communication

Perhaps the most important idea in PBS is that behaviour is communication. When a person cannot easily tell us in words what they need or how they feel, their behaviour speaks for them. Someone who cannot say “I am in pain”, “I am frightened”, or “this is too much for me” may show it instead through their actions.

This is especially true for people who communicate differently — some people with a learning disability, autistic people, or people living with advanced dementia. Behaviour that others find challenging is very often the communication of an unmet need or of distress. Seen this way, your task is not to make the behaviour stop, but to work out what the person is telling you and to help put it right. That is a far kinder and more effective way to support someone.

params.question: In PBS, why is it helpful to see behaviour as communication?

answers.text: Because behaviour often expresses an unmet need or distress that the person cannot easily put into words

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Seeing behaviour as communication helps you ask what the person needs, rather than just trying to stop it.

answers.text: Because it proves the person is behaving badly on purpose

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. PBS does not treat behaviour as deliberate bad behaviour; it treats it as meaningful communication of a need.

answers.text: Because it means you can ignore the behaviour safely

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Not quite. Communication should be understood and responded to, not ignored.

answers.text: Because it shows the behaviour is just part of the person's condition and cannot change

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Behaviour is not simply part of a condition; it has a function, and meeting the need behind it can change things.
